description: This figure presents a corrosion schematic diagram illustrating the degradation mechanisms of Fe–30Mn–8Al–C–xCu low-density steels (where x = 0, 1, and 3 wt%, labeled 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu) during exposure to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The left panels show cross-sectional views contrasting early- and late-stage corrosion processes, while the right panels display three-dimensional corroded surface morphologies. For 0Cu, carbides attached to the matrix surface create lattice distortion that initiates slight localized pitting. For 1Cu, Cu-induced refinement produces many fine carbides distributed across the surface, generating numerous local galvanic cells and a higher density of pits. For 3Cu, undissolved elemental Cu particles enriched at grain boundaries synergize with carbides to drive severe microgalvanic corrosion, resulting in pronounced intergranular attack. Below the schematics, reaction equations summarize the formation of FeOOH, Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄, Al₂O₃, MnO, MnO₂, and Cu₂O. The figure supports the conclusion that increasing Cu content progressively degrades corrosion resistance by shifting the dominant failure mode from isolated carbide-induced pitting to extensive Cu-carbide synergistic intergranular corrosion.
